audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/itch-setup-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-4cu4_lng...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-4cu4_lng
Analyzing itch-setup-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-4cu4_lng) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The `source_x86_64` array contains a URL with a command substitution using `$(date +%F-%H)`, which will be executed during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD when running `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild`. This means that every time the PKGBUILD is sourced, the `date` command is run to generate a timestamp-based filename for the download URL.

While `date` itself is benign, **command substitution in the top-level scope of a PKGBUILD** is dangerous because it allows arbitrary code execution during parsing. An attacker could manipulate such constructs (e.g., through shell injection if the format string were dynamic or user-controlled) or pair it with other obfuscated logic to hide malicious payloads. Even in this case, where it appears intended to create a cache-busting filename, **executing commands during PKGBUILD sourcing violates secure packaging principles**.

More critically, the URL points to `https://itch.io/app/download?platform=linux`, which is **not a pinned, versioned release** but a redirect endpoint that could serve different content over time. Combined with command execution at parse time and lack of checksum validation on this particular source (it's missing from `sha256sums_x86_64` as written), this creates a supply-chain risk where unverified, time-dependent binaries are fetched based on dynamically generated names. This pattern can be abused to leak build-time data or fetch attacker-controlled payloads.

```bash
source_x86_64=("itch-setup-$(date +%F-%H)-amd64::https://itch.io/app/download?platform=linux")
```
</details>
<summary>Command substitution in source enables arbitrary code execution during PKGBUILD parse</summary>
</security_assessment>

x Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Command substitution in source enables arbitrary code execution during PKGBUILD parse[/]

x Initial PKGBUILD check doesn't look good: Command substitution in source enables arbitrary code execution during PKGBUILD parse
Audit complete! Result: Unsafe -- DO NOT INSTALL!
# Issues (1 total)

## PKGBUILD

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Command substitution in source enables arbitrary code execution during PKGBUILD parse

Details:

The `source_x86_64` array contains a URL with a command substitution using `$(date +%F-%H)`, which will be executed during the sourcing of the PKGBUILD when running `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild`. This means that every time the PKGBUILD is sourced, the `date` command is run to generate a timestamp-based filename for the download URL.

While `date` itself is benign, **command substitution in the top-level scope of a PKGBUILD** is dangerous because it allows arbitrary code execution during parsing. An attacker could manipulate such constructs (e.g., through shell injection if the format string were dynamic or user-controlled) or pair it with other obfuscated logic to hide malicious payloads. Even in this case, where it appears intended to create a cache-busting filename, **executing commands during PKGBUILD sourcing violates secure packaging principles**.

More critically, the URL points to `https://itch.io/app/download?platform=linux`, which is **not a pinned, versioned release** but a redirect endpoint that could serve different content over time. Combined with command execution at parse time and lack of checksum validation on this particular source (it's missing from `sha256sums_x86_64` as written), this creates a supply-chain risk where unverified, time-dependent binaries are fetched based on dynamically generated names. This pattern can be abused to leak build-time data or fetch attacker-controlled payloads.

```bash
source_x86_64=("itch-setup-$(date +%F-%H)-amd64::https://itch.io/app/download?platform=linux")
```

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains no dangerous top-level code or `pkgver()` function. The only command substitution is `$(date +%F-%H)` in the `source_x86_64` URL, which generates a timestamp string and is benign. All source URLs point to official project repositories (GitHub and itch.io). No obfuscation, exfiltration, or unexpected system commands are present at the global scope. Since `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` are not executed during the requested commands, they are out of scope for this gate.
</details>
<summary>Safe; no dangerous top-level code or source execution.</summary>
</security_assessment>

It contains no obfuscation, no network requests, no file manipulation outside of running the intended application, and no use of dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, etc.). This is standard packaging practice for a launcher script.

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This script is a standard CI workflow helper for signing and notarizing a macOS app bundle. It reads environment variables Apple ID, app-specific password, and team ID — required by Apple's notarization service — and uses trusted npm packages (`@electron/osx-sign`, `@electron/notarize`, `@itchio/bob`) to perform codesigning and notarization. The entitlements file grants only standard permissions (unsigned executable memory, network client, user-selected file read/write). There are no suspicious network requests, obfuscated code, file exfiltration, or deviations from expected packaging/CI behavior. The script is entirely inline with the upstream project's build process and poses no security threat.
</details>
<summary>Standard macOS signing script, no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

It defines entry points, aliases, loaders, and build options for both the main and renderer processes. There are no network requests, obfuscated code, eval statements, unexpected file operations, or any deviation from normal build tooling configuration. The only external references are to standard Node.js modules (path, url) and local source files. The `inject: ["./esbuild-shims.js"]` line refers to a local shim file, which is a common practice. No evidence of supply-chain attack or malicious behavior.

<summary>

This file is a standard build script for an Electron application using esbuild. It performs routine tasks: cleaning output directories, building main and renderer processes, writing metafiles, and injecting a Content-Security-Policy meta tag into HTML. The CSP includes URLs belonging to the application's own upstream (itch.zone, dale.itch.zone) and localhost for development, which is expected. There are no network requests, no obfuscated code, no dangerous commands like eval/curl/wget, and no access to sensitive system files. The script does exactly what a build step should do and shows no evidence of malicious or injected code.
</details>
<summary>Build script for itch-setup; no malicious behavior.</summary>
